q: What end-to-end pipeline does a production voice AI agent use to handle a real-time customer call?
a: The pipeline is: telephony (SIP/WebRTC) → ASR (speech-to-text) → turn detection → LLM reasoning → TTS (text-to-speech) → audio playout. Each stage adds latency. A production voice AI agent must keep the full round-trip under ~1.5 seconds perceived delay: ASR adds ~200 ms, LLM first-token latency adds ~300-600 ms with streaming, TTS start adds ~100-200 ms. Platforms like Sierra and Decagon are built on top of these pipelines with additional guardrails layers. Contact-center vendors like Genesys and Five9 expose ASR→LLM→TTS hooks via their AI integration SDKs so enterprises can plug in custom voice AI agents without replacing their telephony stack.
fj: You just connect a mic to GPT-4 and it's like chatbots but with audio.
fs: Five distinct stages (telephony, ASR, turn detection, LLM, TTS) each budget latency. Production voice AI stacks stream from ASR through TTS and overlap stages to stay under 1.5 s.

followups.q: How do you overlap ASR and LLM to reduce latency?
followups.a: You stream ASR partial transcripts to the LLM as soon as a high-confidence hypothesis is available, typically above 0.8 confidence on the final segment. The LLM begins prefill before the ASR emits the final transcript. Similarly, TTS starts rendering the first sentence before the LLM finishes generating the full response. This pipelining cuts perceived latency by 200-400 ms on median utterances.

followups.q: What is barge-in and why is it hard in voice AI?
followups.a: Barge-in is when the caller speaks while the agent is still playing audio, interrupting it. Handling it requires continuous ASR even while TTS is playing, echo cancellation to suppress the agent's own voice from the ASR input, and a VAD (voice activity detector) trigger that signals the orchestrator to stop TTS mid-sentence. The hard part is false barge-in from background noise, which causes premature cutoffs.

q: How does Sierra's AI customer service platform differ architecturally from a traditional IVR?
a: Traditional IVR uses DTMF menus and scripted speech grammars (SRGS/VoiceXML) — every path is explicitly authored. Sierra is a voice AI platform built on LLMs where the agent reasons dynamically rather than traversing a decision tree. Sierra's architecture centers on goal-directed agents: you give the agent a policy (what it can and cannot do), a set of tools (CRM lookup, order update, refund API), and a persona. The LLM decides which tool to call. Sierra adds guardrails enforcement — the agent cannot offer discounts above a threshold — and escalation logic as first-class constructs, not IVR branches. Unlike earlier chatbots that required scripted flows, Sierra agents handle open-ended conversation. The measurable difference is containment rate: Sierra customers report 40-60% self-service containment versus 15-25% for DTMF IVR on the same call types.
fj: Sierra is just a chatbot with a voice skin on top of an old IVR.
fs: Sierra replaces scripted grammar trees with a policy-driven LLM agent that reasons over tools. Containment, guardrails, and escalation are first-class platform features, not menu branches.

followups.q: What guardrails does Sierra enforce to keep AI customer service agents safe?
followups.a: Sierra enforces policy constraints at inference time: no promises outside authorized actions, no disclosure of PII not belonging to the authenticated caller, monetary thresholds on refunds/credits, and hard blocks on certain topics (legal, medical advice). Guardrails are evaluated as a separate classifier layer on each LLM turn, not just in the system prompt, so they survive prompt injection attempts.

q: Explain how Genesys Cloud integrates AI into its contact-center platform and what the key integration points are.
a: Genesys Cloud is a CCaaS (Contact Center as a Service) platform with native AI integration via Genesys AI (the umbrella brand for Genesys Cloud AI capabilities, including Dialog Engine Bot Flows and integration with external LLMs). Genesys supports both chatbots on digital channels and voicebots on voice channels from a unified routing engine. Key integration points: (1) Bot Flows — Genesys's own NLU-based chatbot/voicebot builder for containment before live agent; (2) Bring-Your-Own-Bot (BYOB) via AWS Lex connector or generic webhook for integrating external voice AI agents; (3) Agent Assist — real-time transcription + LLM-generated suggestions served to the live agent desktop during a call; (4) Predictive Routing — ML model that matches calls to agents based on outcome probability. For voice AI teams, the most common integration in 2025-2026 is BYOB webhook: your voice AI agent handles the call, and Genesys manages escalation handoff over a SIP transfer or API.
fj: Genesys just transcribes calls — it doesn't do AI.
fs: Genesys has four AI layers: Bot Flows (containment), BYOB hooks (external voice AI), Agent Assist (LLM sidebar for live agents), and Predictive Routing. Most teams plug voice AI agents in via the BYOB webhook.

followups.q: How do you hand off a call from a voice AI agent to a live agent in Genesys without the customer noticing a seam?
followups.a: The handoff uses a warm transfer: the voice AI agent stays on the line during the transition, a Genesys ACD queue is invoked via API with a context payload (transcript, intent, CRM data), and a 'hold music' period of 2-4 seconds masks the connection switch. The live agent desktop shows the full transcript from the voice AI agent before they speak, so they don't re-ask resolved questions. The customer hears a single brief hold rather than a cold transfer silence.

q: Five9 added AI capabilities in 2024-2025. What are the main AI features and how do data engineers typically feed them?
a: Five9's Intelligent CX Cloud gained several AI layers supporting both chatbots on digital channels and voice AI on inbound calls: (1) Five9 Genius AI — NLP-powered IVR intent detection replacing DTMF menus; (2) Agent Assist — real-time LLM suggestions using Verint or Google CCAI integration; (3) Workflow Automation powered by AI routing decisions; (4) Post-call Analytics using ASR transcripts for QA scoring. Data engineers feed these by building pipelines that push CRM events (order status, account flags) into Five9's real-time data connectors, so the voice AI agent has fresh context. Event-driven patterns are preferred: a Kafka topic for order updates that the Five9 webhook subscribes to, giving the voice AI agent sub-second context freshness. For analytics, Five9 exports call transcripts to an S3 bucket which is ingested into Databricks or Snowflake for CSAT prediction and deflection dashboards.
fj: Five9 is just an old telephony vendor, it doesn't do AI.
fs: Five9 Genius AI handles intent detection, Agent Assist, workflow routing, and post-call QA. Data teams wire it with Kafka for live context and S3 exports for analytics pipelines.

followups.q: How do you calculate deflection rate and containment rate for a Five9 + AI deployment?
followups.a: Deflection rate = calls fully resolved by voice AI without ever entering the ACD queue / total inbound calls. Containment rate is slightly broader: calls resolved by the voice AI OR bot without live-agent intervention, including calls that entered the queue but were resolved by the bot before an agent picked up. Five9's reporting API exposes these as 'handled by IVR' vs 'transferred' dispositions. You layer in post-call survey CSAT to verify true resolution, since a deflected call with a re-call within 24 hours is a false containment.

q: Decagon builds AI agents for customer support. How does their approach to voice AI and chatbots differ from general-purpose LLM deployment?
a: Decagon's platform is purpose-built for customer-support AI agents — both chatbots and voicebots. The key architectural differentiators: (1) Knowledge grounding — Decagon connects directly to company knowledge bases, Zendesk/Salesforce help centers, and product docs, performing RAG with citations, so the agent never hallucinates policies; (2) Action execution — agents can actually perform CRM writes (cancel subscription, issue refund) via OAuth-authorized integrations, not just provide answers; (3) Escalation detection — a separate classifier monitors frustration signals (repeated negation, 'let me speak to a human') and triggers escalation before the customer has to explicitly ask; (4) Compliance mode — all responses are logged, all actions are audited, which is critical for financial services and healthcare Decagon targets. For voice AI specifically, Decagon uses streaming TTS and barge-in detection on top of their chat agent core, making the same policy and tools available across channels.
fj: Decagon is just a wrapper around ChatGPT for support tickets.
fs: Decagon differentiates on knowledge grounding (RAG from help centers), action execution (CRM writes), proactive escalation detection, and full auditability — not just LLM chat.

followups.q: How does Decagon handle a customer asking an AI agent about a sensitive topic like a billing dispute it can't resolve?
followups.a: Decagon's agents apply a policy constraint layer: if the intent is classified as beyond authorized action scope, the agent acknowledges the request, summarizes what it can offer (e.g., log the dispute), and offers escalation to a specialist queue. The agent never fabricates a resolution or makes promises outside its action set. The escalation carries a structured handoff payload with the full interaction transcript and extracted intent, so the human agent has full context immediately.

q: What is the latency budget for a real-time voice AI agent and how do you measure and enforce it?
a: The target total round-trip latency for a voice AI agent is ≤1,500 ms from end of caller utterance to first TTS audio byte played. Budget breakdown in a well-tuned stack: VAD end-of-turn detection ~50-100 ms, ASR final transcript ~150-250 ms, LLM first token (streaming) ~250-500 ms, TTS first audio chunk ~100-150 ms, network+audio buffering ~50-100 ms. Total ~600-1,100 ms median with budget to spare. Enforcement: instrument each stage with OpenTelemetry spans tagged with call_id. Alert on p95 > 1,200 ms at any stage. Common latency killers: ASR silence padding (set endpointing sensitivity aggressively), LLM context length (trim old turns), TTS model size (use a streaming-capable smaller TTS model like Cartesia or ElevenLabs Turbo v2).
fj: Voice AI latency doesn't matter much — callers expect some delay.
fs: The 1,500 ms end-to-end budget splits across VAD, ASR, LLM first-token, and TTS first-chunk. Instrument with OTel spans per call_id and alert on p95 stage latency.

followups.q: What TTS providers are typically used in production voice AI agents and what are the tradeoffs?
followups.a: The main choices in early 2026: ElevenLabs (highest quality, ~120 ms first chunk with Turbo v2, ~$0.15/1k chars), Cartesia Sonic (low latency ~80 ms, good quality, cheaper), AWS Polly (cheap, acceptable quality, Neural voices), Google Cloud TTS (competitive latency). For voice AI agents where naturalness matters (Sierra/Decagon-style), ElevenLabs or Cartesia are typical. For cost-sensitive high-volume IVR integrations on Genesys/Five9, AWS Polly or Google TTS are common.

q: How do you implement barge-in detection in a voice AI pipeline and what are the failure modes?
a: Barge-in allows the caller to interrupt the voice AI agent's speech. Implementation steps: (1) run ASR and VAD continuously on the caller audio stream even while TTS is playing; (2) apply acoustic echo cancellation (AEC) to subtract the TTS audio from the microphone signal before feeding it to ASR, preventing the agent from detecting its own speech as caller speech; (3) when VAD detects caller speech energy above threshold, send a barge-in interrupt signal to the TTS player and LLM orchestrator; (4) flush the TTS buffer and start a new ASR recognition context. Failure modes: (a) false barge-in from background noise — mitigated by tuning VAD sensitivity and requiring sustained energy > 200 ms; (b) AEC under-cancellation when the caller and agent speak at very close frequencies — use a dual-microphone array model if hardware allows; (c) barge-in loop where TTS itself triggers VAD — AEC is the fix; (d) state machine corruption if the orchestrator doesn't properly abort in-flight LLM generation — cancel the streaming LLM request immediately on barge-in.
fj: Barge-in is just detecting the caller's voice level crossing a threshold.
fs: Barge-in requires AEC + continuous VAD + orchestrator abort. The four failure modes (false positive noise, AEC failure, self-loop, state corruption) each need a specific mitigation.

followups.q: How does barge-in behavior differ between voicebots on Genesys versus a custom ASR→LLM→TTS stack?
followups.a: Genesys handles barge-in at the platform level — their media server runs AEC and VAD natively, and the Bot Flow or BYOB webhook receives a 'BargeIn' event. You don't control the VAD tuning. In a custom stack, you control every parameter: VAD aggressiveness, AEC model, energy threshold, and minimum speech duration before triggering. Custom stacks enable better barge-in behavior for calm, low-noise call center environments but require more tuning for noisy consumer settings.

q: Define deflection rate, containment rate, and resolution rate for chatbots and voice AI agents. How do you set targets for each?
a: Deflection rate: percentage of contacts (calls or chats) handled entirely by the AI agent without any human agent involvement. Containment rate: percentage where the customer's intent was resolved — a superset that includes cases where a human briefly assisted but the AI handled the primary resolution. Resolution rate: percentage where the customer's problem was actually solved, verified by post-interaction survey CSAT or absence of re-contact within 72 hours. Target-setting process: start with your current IVR self-service rate as the baseline. A voice AI agent replacing DTMF IVR typically targets 35-55% deflection in the first 90 days on well-structured intents (order status, account balance). Chatbots on digital channels typically achieve 50-70% deflection since typed language has lower ambiguity than speech. Add guardrails: deflection > 60% with CSAT < 4.0 means you're deflecting but not resolving — the model needs retune or knowledge base gaps addressed.
fj: Deflection and containment are the same thing — calls not going to an agent.
fs: Deflection = no human touched it. Containment = intent resolved (human or bot). Resolution = problem actually solved. Set targets by starting from your IVR self-service baseline and tracking CSAT alongside deflection to catch false containment.

followups.q: How do you distinguish a successful AI agent deflection from a customer who gave up?
followups.a: You track three signals: (1) call/chat end disposition — did the customer close the interaction with an affirmative intent signal or just hang up? (2) re-contact rate — did they call/chat again within 24-72 hours with the same intent? (3) explicit CSAT survey immediately post-interaction. A 'ghost deflection' looks like success in the dashboard but has high re-contact and low CSAT. Combining all three gives true resolution rate, which should be the primary KPI.

q: How do you design escalation-to-human logic for a voice AI agent handling complex customer service calls?
a: Escalation is a first-class design concern for both voice AI agents and chatbots, not an afterthought. A layered escalation policy: (1) Explicit request — detect 'speak to a human / agent / supervisor' intents with a dedicated classifier, not just keyword match; (2) Frustration signals — repeated clarification requests, negative sentiment, rising speech rate, use of profanity — trigger soft escalation offer; (3) Confidence threshold — if the LLM's routing classifier scores below 0.6 for intent, escalate rather than guess; (4) Policy ceiling — actions that exceed the agent's authorized scope (refund > $500, legal dispute, medical emergency) trigger mandatory escalation; (5) Timeout — if the conversation exceeds 3 turns without progress on the stated intent, proactively offer transfer. Escalation hand-off must include: full transcript, extracted intent, caller authentication state, CRM context, and preferred resolution so the human agent doesn't start from scratch. Both Genesys and Five9 support structured CTI screen-pop payloads for this.
fj: Escalation just means pressing 0 to reach an agent — the AI doesn't need special logic.
fs: Escalation has five triggers (explicit request, frustration, low confidence, policy ceiling, timeout) each with different handling. The handoff payload carrying transcript + intent + CRM context is as important as the trigger logic.

followups.q: How do Sierra and Decagon handle escalation differently from Genesys/Five9 routing?
followups.a: Sierra and Decagon own the escalation decision at the AI layer — their agent policy defines when to transfer, and they emit a structured escalation event. Genesys and Five9 receive that event and execute the ACD routing (queue selection, priority, skills-based routing). They're complementary: the AI platform decides 'escalate now with this context' and the CCaaS platform decides 'route to this queue at this priority.' Conflicts arise when both have escalation logic — a governance rule should make the AI platform authoritative for the trigger decision.

q: What are the key ASR accuracy metrics for voice AI in production and how do you improve WER on domain-specific vocabulary?
a: The primary ASR metric is Word Error Rate (WER) = (Substitutions + Deletions + Insertions) / Total Reference Words. ASR errors matter more in voice AI than in chatbots since there's no spell-correct layer — garbage in means the LLM reasons on a corrupted transcript. For customer service voice AI, target WER < 8% on clean audio, < 15% with background noise. But WER is aggregate — track Named-Entity Error Rate separately for product names, account numbers, and addresses, since these are high-stakes misrecognitions. Improvement techniques: (1) Custom language models / vocabulary biasing — both Google STT and AWS Transcribe support phrase hints/boost weights for domain terms; in Whisper-based stacks, fine-tune on domain audio; (2) Context injection — pass the caller's account type and recent intents as metadata to the ASR model if the API supports it; (3) Acoustic model fine-tuning — collect ~50-100 hours of labeled call audio from your specific telephony codec (G.711/G.729 compression significantly degrades standard ASR models trained on 16kHz audio); (4) Confidence filtering — flag low-confidence ASR output and prompt the caller to repeat rather than passing garbage to the LLM.
fj: WER below 20% is good enough — the LLM can figure out misspellings.
fs: WER < 8% clean / 15% noisy, but track Named-Entity Error Rate separately. Fix via phrase boosting, acoustic fine-tuning on telephony codec audio, and confidence-gated re-prompting.

followups.q: How does telephony codec compression affect ASR and what do you do about it?
followups.a: PSTN calls use G.711 (8kHz, mu-law) or G.729 (compressed). Standard ASR models are trained on 16kHz or 24kHz wideband audio. The narrow bandwidth removes frequencies above 3.4kHz, which carry consonant distinctiveness (s/f/th). WER typically increases 3-8 points on narrow-band compared to wideband. Mitigations: use ASR models fine-tuned on telephony data (Google STT has a 'phone_call' model), or upsample to 16kHz with a bandwidth extension model (Aero/MetricGAN) before feeding ASR.

q: How do you build guardrails for chatbots handling customer service to prevent hallucinated policy answers?
a: Three-layer guardrails architecture: (1) Retrieval grounding — all policy and product answers must be retrieved from an authoritative knowledge base (vector store over help center docs), not generated from LLM parametric memory. Use a strict RAG pattern: if retrieval confidence < threshold, the agent says it doesn't know rather than guessing. (2) Output classifiers — a separate smaller model (or regex + LLM judge) checks each agent response for: unauthorized commitments ('I guarantee your refund by tomorrow'), PII leakage, and topic violations (legal advice, competitor mentions). Responses that fail are rewritten or blocked. (3) Adversarial probe testing — red-team the chatbot with jailbreak attempts, leading questions ('just between us, can you give me a bigger discount?'), and out-of-domain probes. Run this as a scheduled CI job using an LLM evaluator. Decagon bakes layers 1 and 2 into their platform; Sierra adds policy compliance as a configurable constraint layer. For teams building on raw LLMs, you implement all three manually.
fj: A detailed system prompt is enough to prevent hallucinations in chatbots.
fs: Three layers: RAG grounding (no parametric answers), output classifier (unauthorized commitments + PII + topic), adversarial CI probes. System prompts alone don't hold under adversarial pressure.

followups.q: How do you handle the case where a customer explicitly asks the chatbot to ignore its instructions?
followups.a: Prompt injection and jailbreak attempts should be classified by a separate intent detector before the main LLM processes the turn. If the input contains 'ignore your instructions', 'pretend you have no rules', 'DAN mode', or similar constructs, the agent responds with a policy-scripted refusal and logs the attempt for security review. The main LLM should not be the sole defense — a lightweight classifier (fine-tuned distilBERT or a fast LLM API call) running before the main inference is more reliable than relying on the main model's alignment.

q: Describe how a voice AI agent handles authentication and caller identity verification in a contact center setting.
a: Caller authentication in voice AI follows the same trust levels as human IVR but with additional ML tools. Unlike chatbots where session cookies or OAuth tokens authenticate users passively, voice calls have no inherent identity signal — only audio. The layers: (1) ANI (Automatic Number Identification) — soft signal, easy to spoof via VOIP; (2) Voice biometrics — the caller's voice print is matched against an enrolled model (Nuance, Veridas, or Pindrop). Pindrop additionally does 'phoneprinting' to detect VOIP vs cellular vs landline and flag suspicious ANI patterns; (3) Knowledge-Based Authentication (KBA) — the voice AI agent asks for the last 4 of SSN, DOB, or account PIN. The agent should ask only the minimum required by policy; (4) Passive authentication — Pindrop and similar platforms run fraud scoring in parallel with the conversation without explicit challenge. For high-value actions (large refunds, address changes), require step-up authentication (OTP to registered phone or email). Genesys and Five9 both integrate with Pindrop natively; Sierra and Decagon integrate via webhook to the enterprise's identity provider.
fj: The AI agent just asks for the account number and that's enough authentication.
fs: Authentication layers: ANI (soft), voice biometrics (Pindrop/Nuance), KBA, passive fraud scoring. High-value actions require step-up auth. Risk of AI agents: they can be socially engineered to skip KBA if not enforced at the platform layer.

followups.q: How does voice cloning risk change the authentication picture for voice AI agents?
followups.a: Voice cloning tools (ElevenLabs, VALL-E 2) can replicate a target voice from short samples publicly available on social media. Biometric voice verification models trained before 2023 were not built to detect synthetic speech and have high false-acceptance rates against clones. Modern voice biometric vendors (Pindrop, Nuance) have added liveness detection that looks for GAN artifacts and codec fingerprints. However, the risk is real enough that leading contact centers are moving toward multi-factor: voice biometrics + OTP, and some are deprioritizing voice-as-sole-factor entirely.

q: How do you evaluate an AI customer service agent's quality at scale without manually reviewing every call?
a: Use LLM-as-Judge evaluation pipelines: (1) Auto-score every call transcript against a rubric: was the intent resolved? Were policy constraints followed? Was the escalation triggered correctly? Was tone appropriate? A GPT-4o or Claude 3.5 Sonnet judge scores each dimension 1-5; (2) Sample human review for calibration — human reviewers score 2-3% of calls, compare to LLM judge scores, and fine-tune the judge prompt when divergence > 0.5 points; (3) Outcome-linked metrics — correlate LLM judge scores with re-contact rate and CSAT to validate the judge is measuring the right things; (4) Segment by intent — 'order status' calls have different quality criteria than 'billing dispute' calls; (5) Track per-model-version — when you deploy a new LLM or new policy prompt, run the evaluator on the first 1,000 post-deployment calls before declaring success. Decagon's platform includes a built-in LLM evaluator; on Sierra you can configure custom quality metrics. For Genesys/Five9 call transcripts, teams typically build this as a separate data pipeline in Databricks or a Python service.
fj: You sample 5% of calls for QA review — same as before AI.
fs: LLM-as-Judge on every transcript + human calibration sample + outcome correlation + per-intent segmentation. 5% human sample gave coverage before; with AI you can score 100% and use humans only for judge calibration.

followups.q: What makes a good LLM judge prompt for scoring AI customer service agent responses?
followups.a: A good judge prompt is specific and rubric-based, not 'was this good?' Provide the reference policy document as context, define each scoring dimension with examples of 1 and 5 scores, instruct the judge to cite a specific span from the transcript for each score, and use chain-of-thought before the numeric score to reduce positivity bias. Chain-of-thought scoring has 15-20% lower variance against human scores than direct numeric scoring.

q: What data pipeline do you build to feed real-time context to a voice AI agent during a live call?
a: The live context pipeline must deliver sub-200 ms from event to agent-readable context. Architecture: (1) Trigger events — telephony platform (Genesys/Five9) emits a CTI event at call start with ANI/DNIS; (2) Real-time lookup — a sidecar service does parallel async fetches: CRM record (Salesforce/ServiceNow), recent order history, open ticket status, subscription tier. Fetches must complete in ≤150 ms — use caching (Redis, 5-minute TTL) for frequently accessed records and async fan-out; (3) Context assembly — structured JSON payload injected as system message context before the first LLM turn; (4) Mid-call updates — for long calls, subscribe to CRM webhooks so if an order ships mid-call the agent knows; (5) Privacy filtering — strip PII fields not needed for the call intent before injection. On Genesys, this is implemented as a Data Action called at IVR entry; on Five9 as a Pre-Queue script. For Sierra and Decagon, you register the context as a tool the agent calls, or pass it as part of the session initialization payload.
fj: The AI agent can look up the customer's info whenever it needs it mid-conversation.
fs: Context must arrive before the first LLM turn: parallel async CRM/order lookups in ≤150 ms, Redis-cached, assembled as system-message JSON, with mid-call webhook subscriptions for long calls.
